beats.text: An autoregressive model formalizes a simple idea: predict the next value by regressing the series on its own past.

visual.sub: Tomorrow looks like today, plus a nudge

beats.text: AR of order p predicts the current value as a weighted sum of the previous p values plus a fresh white-noise shock.
For AR(1) the coefficient phi is the one-step persistence: near one means shocks linger, near zero means fast mean reversion.
The process is stationary only when every root of its characteristic polynomial lies strictly outside the unit circle.
For an AR(2) with phi-one 0.5 and phi-two 0.3 the roots are 1.17 and minus 2.84, both outside the circle, so it is stationary.
The Yule-Walker equations recover the coefficients in closed form by solving a linear system built from the autocorrelations.
Where an AR model remembers past values, a moving-average model remembers past shocks instead.
MA of order q writes the current value as the mean plus the current shock and a weighted sum of the last q shocks.
An MA process has finite memory: a shock affects only the next q observations, then vanishes completely.
That sharp autocorrelation cutoff after lag q is the fingerprint used to identify the MA order from data.
AR and MA are mirror images: an invertible MA equals an infinite AR, and a stationary AR equals an infinite MA.
The diagnostic rule: ACF cutting off signals MA, PACF cutting off signals AR, and both tailing off signals mixed ARMA.

visual.text: ACF cutoff -> MA  ·  PACF cutoff -> AR

beats.text: Combine both memories and you get ARMA: the value depends on its own past and on past shocks together.
In lag-operator form an AR polynomial acting on the values equals an MA polynomial acting on the noise.
ARMA needs a stationary series, but most real data trends or wanders, so the I in ARIMA differences it first.
Three integers fully specify the model: p autoregressive terms, d differences, and q moving-average terms.
ARIMA(0,1,0) is the random walk: each value equals the previous one plus an unpredictable shock.
Its best forecast is the last observation, and uncertainty grows like the square root of the horizon as variance accumulates.
A plain ARIMA can chase a trend but has no machinery for a repeating season like a yearly or weekly cycle.

visual.sub: A second ARIMA at the seasonal lag

beats.text: SARIMA bolts a second seasonal ARIMA onto the first, applying the same AR, I, and MA logic at the seasonal lag s.
Seasonal differencing subtracts the value one full season ago, so monthly data with a yearly cycle uses lag twelve.
Six orders replace three: the lowercase terms handle short-range dynamics, the uppercase terms handle seasonal dynamics.
The airline model, SARIMA(0,1,1)(0,1,1) with s twelve, fits a startling range of monthly business series.

visual.sub: The airline model: the seasonal baseline to beat

beats.text: SARIMA assumes one fixed period with constant amplitude, so overlapping or growing seasonality calls for other tools.
Box and Jenkins proposed not just models but a procedure: an iterative loop that turns a raw series into a fitted forecast.
The loop cycles through three stages until the residuals are indistinguishable from white noise.
Identification competes candidate orders on an information criterion that rewards fit and penalizes added complexity.
The complexity penalty two-k is the engine inside auto-dot-arima, which searches orders and keeps the lowest-AIC model.
A low AIC with autocorrelated residuals is still a failed model, so the white-noise residual check is the real gate.
Four common failures recur: over-differencing, reading correlograms too literally, trusting AIC over residuals, and over-extrapolating.
